title: Growing calendula
subtitle: Pot marigold from seed: the easy edible-flower starter
excerpt: Calendula officinalis (pot marigold) is the gateway edible flower for a UK garden. Direct-sown in March or April, the plants flower from June to first frost, self-seed into next year's bed, and the petals colour rice, butter and salads.
sourceNotes: RHS calendula page (https://www.rhs.org.uk/plants/2999/calendula-officinalis/details) for sowing window, deadheading practice, and hardiness rating. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, on the use of calendula petals in dairy and rice dishes. Gertrude Jekyll, Wood and Garden (1899), Project Gutenberg, on calendula as a self-seeding cottage-garden annual.

glossaryTerms.definition: Removing spent flowers before the plant sets seed. The plant interprets the missing seed-head as a signal to flower again, deadheading extends the flowering season, often substantially. Routine on annual flowers grown for long display.

An annual plant's habit of dropping ripe seed into the surrounding soil at the end of its life, producing the next generation of plants without the gardener's intervention. Calendula, borage, nasturtium and forget-me-not are all reliable self-seeders in a UK garden.

content.text: Calendula officinalis (the pot marigold) is one of the most generous flowers a UK garden grows. Sown direct in March or April, the plants are flowering by June, and a single sowing carries through to the first hard frost in late October or November. The petals are edible and have been used in British and European kitchens for centuries; the plants self-seed reliably, so a single bed established in year one returns of its own accord every spring after.

This guide covers the full growing arc from sowing to seed-saving. Calendula is one of the easier-to-grow flowering annuals if the conditions are right: full sun, sharp drainage, and a soil that isn't over-rich. Rich soil produces lush foliage and few flowers, which is the most common cause of disappointment with a calendula bed.
Choosing a position

Full sun, ideally six hours of direct light a day or more. Calendula tolerates partial shade and crops in it, but the flower count is lower and the stems lean toward the brightest aspect. Soil should be free-draining; sandy or loamy soils suit the plant best. Heavy clay sites grow calendula reasonably if drainage is improved with grit at planting, but the plants are shorter-lived.

Calendula thrives in cooler temperatures and lower soil fertility than most kitchen-garden flowers. A bed that was previously a vegetable bed and is now resting on a fallow year is ideal; a bed freshly enriched with manure or compost produces tall leggy plants with few flowers. If the soil is rich, plant calendula at the back of the bed where the leafy growth won't crowd shorter neighbours.

Container culture works well, a 20 cm pot of John Innes No 1 or 2 (the low-fertility composts) holds one plant comfortably. Calendula in pots flowers earlier than in the open ground because the soil warms faster, which is useful for the windowsill grower.
Planting out

Calendula seed is large enough to handle individually and germinates reliably outdoors once the soil reaches 10°C, late March in the south, early to mid-April further north. Direct-sow at 1 cm depth, 30 cm apart in the final position. Cover lightly with soil, firm, and water in. Germination takes 7-14 days.

An alternative route is module-tray sowing in February under cover, planted out in April. The earlier route flowers a fortnight earlier but the plants are no stronger by July than direct-sown ones, and the extra work doesn't usually justify itself for a flower this easy from seed.

Successional sowing every three weeks from March to early June extends the peak-flowering window, a March sowing is in full flower by mid-June; an April sowing carries through July and August; a May sowing comes into its own in September. The plants are short-lived in flower (8-12 weeks of peak production per plant) so the staggered sowings sustain a steady picking supply.

An autumn sowing in September overwinters as small rosettes and flowers a fortnight earlier than spring-sown plants the following year, useful for cottage-garden beds where calendula is part of the long-term planting.
Through the season

Watering: moderate. Calendula tolerates dry spells once established and resents waterlogging; let the top 2 cm of soil dry between waterings in normal UK summer conditions. In a hot dry week, a thorough soak twice a week is plenty; in a wet July, the plants need no extra water at all.
Feeding: skip it. Calendula in moderately-fertile soil flowers more freely than calendula in rich soil. The plant doesn't need a feeding routine; high-nitrogen feeds positively reduce flowering.
Once the plants are flowering (June onwards),
deadheading

extends the season substantially. Snip spent flower-heads back to the next pair of leaves every few days; the plant responds by producing more flower buds rather than putting energy into seed production. A bed deadheaded twice a week flowers through to first frost; an undeadheaded bed shuts down in early September having set seed.

Powdery mildew is the one disease that hits calendula reliably in late summer, silvery patches on the leaves spread through the lower foliage. Ventilation reduces it (don't crowd the plants); milk-water sprays are the traditional home remedy and have some research support. Severely-affected plants can be pulled and composted; the disease doesn't carry into the soil.
Harvest

For edible petals, pick the flowers when they're fully open in mid-morning, after the dew has dried but before the heat of the day. Hold the flower head and pull the petals out; they release cleanly. Use the petals fresh in salads, rice (the petals were the cheap saffron of the British kitchen historically), butter, or compound oils.

Drying the petals for storage: spread them in a single layer on a paper-lined tray, away from direct sun, in a warm dry room. Three or four days to crisp-dry; store in airtight jars in a cool dark cupboard. Dried petals keep their colour for around a year before fading; replace annually.

For cut flowers, harvest the whole stem at first morning light when the flower is just opening. Strip the lower leaves, sear the stem-end in just-boiled water for ten seconds, then plunge into cold water and arrange. Vase life is 5-7 days.
Saving seed and self-seeding
Calendula is one of the most reliable
self-seeders

in a UK garden. Leave a few flower heads in late August to ripen on the plant; the curled, crescent-shaped seeds drop into the soil through September and October, overwinter, and germinate the following spring without any intervention.

For controlled seed-saving, cut whole seed heads when the seeds have dried to brown but before they shatter. Rub the heads between palms over a tray to release the seeds. Store in a paper envelope in a cool dry place; viability is 3-4 years.
What can go wrong

attrs.heading: If something went sideways
attrs.intro: Three common failures in calendula growing and what to do.

items.symptom: Tall leggy plants with few flowers
items.cause: Soil too rich, or partial shade.
items.fix: Move to a sunnier, less-fertile bed. Don't add compost or manure to a calendula bed. If the plants are already in place and leggy, pinch out the growing tip at 20 cm to force branching.

items.cause: Plants setting seed because deadheading lapsed.
items.fix: Deadhead twice a week from first flower. If the plants have already shut down, pull them and replant from a later successional sowing.

items.symptom: Silvery patches on leaves spreading through the plant
items.cause: Powdery mildew, common in late summer in warm dry conditions.
items.fix: Improve airflow (don't crowd plants). A milk-water spray (1:9 milk to water, weekly) helps. Severely affected plants can be pulled; the disease doesn't persist in the soil.

content.text: Variety pointers

Plain Calendula officinalis seed (single orange flowers, the classic pot marigold) is the cheapest and the historic norm; named varieties expand the range. Indian Prince has deep orange flowers with dark centres; Snow Princess is cream-yellow. The double-flowered cultivars (Pacific Beauty, Art Shades) look striking but produce less seed and are harder to deadhead. The species is the practical starting point.

A note on calendula and herbal use: the petals are food-grade and the historic culinary use is well-documented. Therapeutic claims for calendula belong in the Herbal category, where the dose, preparation method, and safety frame can carry the claim. This Garden guide stops at the kitchen.
